title: Spoofed Email Detection
dataSources: DMARC aggregate reports, email authentication logs
spl:
index=email sourcetype="dmarc:aggregate"
| where dkim_result!="pass" OR spf_result!="pass"
| stats count by src, header_from, dkim_result, spf_result, disposition
| sort -count
description: DMARC/SPF/DKIM failures indicate email spoofing attempts. Monitoring validates email authentication configuration.
value: DMARC/SPF/DKIM failures indicate email spoofing attempts. Monitoring validates email authentication configuration.
implementation: Configure DMARC reporting (aggregate to a designated mailbox). Ingest DMARC XML reports. Track authentication failures by sending domain. Alert on spoofing of your own domains. Move toward DMARC p=reject for full protection.
visualization: Table (authentication failures), Bar chart (failures by domain), Pie chart (pass vs fail), Line chart (spoofing trend).
cimSpl:
| tstats `summariesonly` count
  from datamodel=Email.All_Email
  where (All_Email.message_info="*spf*" OR All_Email.message_info="*dkim*" OR All_Email.message_info="*dmarc*" OR All_Email.signature="*spoof*")
  by All_Email.src_user All_Email.recipient span=1h
| sort -count
knownFalsePositives: SPF, DKIM, and DMARC failures from legitimate forwarders, mailing lists, email aliases, poorly configured but valid senders, and domains during M&A or DNS transitions.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
‚Ä¢ Install and configure the required add-on or app: Email security TA.
‚Ä¢ Ensure the following data sources are available: DMARC aggregate reports, email authentication logs.
‚Ä¢ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 ‚Äî Configure data collection
Configure DMARC reporting (aggregate to a designated mailbox). Ingest DMARC XML reports. Track authentication failures by sending domain. Alert on spoofing of your own domains. Move toward DMARC p=reject for full protection.

Step 2 ‚Äî Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=email sourcetype="dmarc:aggregate"
| where dkim_result!="pass" OR spf_result!="pass"
| stats count by src, header_from, dkim_result, spf_result, disposition
| sort -count
```

Understanding this SPL

**Spoofed Email Detection** ‚Äî DMARC/SPF/DKIM failures indicate email spoofing attempts. Monitoring validates email authentication configuration.

Documented **Data sources**: DMARC aggregate reports, email authentication logs. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Email security TA. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚Äîrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: email; **sourcetype**: dmarc:aggregate. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚Ä¢ Scopes the data: index=email, sourcetype="dmarc:aggregate". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
‚Ä¢ Filters the current rows with `where dkim_result!="pass" OR spf_result!="pass"` ‚Äî typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
‚Ä¢ `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by src, header_from, dkim_result, spf_result, disposition** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
‚Ä¢ Orders rows with `sort` ‚Äî combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats `summariesonly` count
  from datamodel=Email.All_Email
  where (All_Email.message_info="*spf*" OR All_Email.message_info="*dkim*" OR All_Email.message_info="*dmarc*" OR All_Email.signature="*spoof*")
  by All_Email.src_user All_Email.recipient span=1h
| sort -count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**Spoofed Email Detection** ‚Äî DMARC/SPF/DKIM failures indicate email spoofing attempts. Monitoring validates email authentication configuration.

Documented **Data sources**: DMARC aggregate reports, email authentication logs. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Email security TA. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚Äîrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚Ä¢ Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Email.All_Email` ‚Äî enable acceleration for that model.
‚Ä¢ Orders rows with `sort` ‚Äî combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 ‚Äî Validate
Compare search results in Splunk with the Microsoft 365 Defender portal (https://security.microsoft.com) and, where applicable, the Proofpoint TAP dashboard, Mimecast Personal Portal, or Cisco ESA `mail_logs`. Confirm events are present, fields parse correctly, and sample ratios match the vendor console for the same time range.

Step 4 ‚Äî Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (authentication failures), Bar chart (failures by domain), Pie chart (pass vs fail), Line chart (spoofing trend).
grandmaExplanation: We watch for mail that fails or weakens sender authentication, so spoofing and lookalike senders are caught and we can keep improving DMARC, SPF, and DKIM alignment.
dataModelAcceleration: Enable Data Model Acceleration for the CIM `Email.All_Email` dataset once message and attachment fields are CIM-tagged; otherwise `cimSpl` tstats may return no rows.
